fault_hypothesis: Line 67 uses strict greater-than (`now > effective_ttl`) instead of greater-than-or-equal (`now >= effective_ttl`), so when `now` exactly equals the TTL boundary the token is incorrectly returned instead of treated as expired.

constraints.intent: At line 67, if now equals effective_ttl the condition should be true (expired), but `now > effective_ttl` misses the equality case — check that when now == effective_ttl the branch is taken (returns None).
After computing effective_ttl, verify that the expiration check uses >= semantics: when now equals effective_ttl, the token must be considered expired.
Before using effective_ttl in the comparison, assert that when now equals effective_ttl the strict-greater-than condition is false (revealing the off-by-one boundary bug).
In the else branch of the expiration check, now must be strictly less than effective_ttl for the token to be legitimately valid; equality should have triggered expiration.
